lat: vir ille summus nullus imperator fuit
eng: that great man was no general at all
alternatives: that supreme man was no commander at all
that highest of men was no general at all
note: Nullus is the Latin emphatic negation of identity --- not `not at all a' but `no general at all'. Rendering with `no ... at all' preserves the bite. The line is among the most famous Cicero ever wrote on Pompey.

lat: ubi non sis qui fueris, non esse cur velis vivere
eng: when you are not the man you were, there is no reason to want to live
alternatives: when you are not what you used to be, there is no reason to wish to live
note: Cicero calls this an `old saying' (vetus est enim); he likely has a Greek tag in mind, perhaps from comedy. Rendering directly preserves the doubled `you / you' that makes the line memorable.

eng: in the power of our friends
alternatives: in the hands of our friends
in the control of our friends
note: Friends here is the standard Ciceronian euphemism for the triumvirs after Luca. Power preserves the political-control register that hands or control would soften.

lat: tenent omnia idque ita omnis intellegere volunt
eng: they hold everything, and they want everyone to know it
alternatives: they have the whole thing in hand, and they want all to understand that
note: The point is the doubled tenent / volunt: not just dominance but the visible display of dominance. The English shortens to keep the punch.

eng: scornful of Syria, blustering about Spain
alternatives: disdaining Syria, vaunting about Spain
spurning Syria, boasting of Spain
note: The two participles balanced by tense and tone: Pompey speaks of his rival's province with feigned contempt, of his own with hollow swagger. `Blustering' (for iactans) catches the hollowness Cicero is signalling.

lat: in illa tua sedecula quam habes sub imagine Aristotelis
eng: in that little chair of yours that you have under the bust of Aristotle
alternatives: in that little seat of yours under the image of Aristotle
note: Sedecula is the diminutive of sedes; Atticus's reading-chair, located under the imago (likely a herm or bust) of Aristotle, is one of the most cherished domestic images in the corpus. `Bust' for imago because the English `image' loses the visual specificity.

eng: on condition the elections actually hold
alternatives: if the elections actually take place
supposing the elections hold
note: ut sit rata is parenthetical: a conditional wish that the comitia actually be ratified. Cicero is not sorry to have missed the senate's altercations — provided the elections do happen and his absence is not a forfeit. Reading follows Shackleton Bailey.

alternatives: the present (lady)
for the present occasion
note: tēn parousan is feminine accusative singular: "the (lady) present." Conventional reading: Pilia, Atticus's wife. Cicero asks that Atticus arrange for Pilia to be at home so he does not arrive as a stranger. The Greek is the discreet hint.

eng: many flashes of genius, yet much craft as well
alternatives: many flashes of genius, yet of no great craft
note: Textual crux: Perseus carries inultae (corrupt). Watt OCT prints multae tamen artis = "yet of much craft also"; Shackleton Bailey prefers non multae tamen artis = "yet of no great craft." The two readings carry opposite verdicts on Lucretius. Going with the OCT reading as the safer general consensus and the more probably correct sense (Cicero is praising both genius and craft, with tamen marking the pair).

lat: virum te putabo si Sallusti Empedoclea legeris, hominem non putabo
eng: I shall count you a man if you read Sallustius's Empedoclea; a human being I shall not count you
alternatives: I shall reckon you a hero for reading it, but no human being
note: The pun is vir / homo: superhuman in stamina (vir), but inhuman in feeling (non homo). The English keeps the two words distinct.

alternatives: seize some chariot
snatch up an essedum
note: essedum is the British and Gallic two-horse light war-chariot, much remarked on by Caesar's officers in De Bello Gallico 4. Cicero's joke: if Britain has no plunder, Trebatius can ride one of those home. "War-chariot" preserves the Gallic-British specificity "chariot" alone would lose.

eng: you are urging on a runner
alternatives: you are urging on a man already running
you are spurring a runner
note: Standard Latin idiom: to encourage someone already moving in the desired direction. The English keeps the metaphor intact for the long horse-and-chariot extension that follows.

eng: softer than the lowest little ear-tip
alternatives: softer than the very tip of an ear-lobe
note: oricula is the diminutive of oris (auris): the little ear, the ear-tip; oricula infima the lowest, softest part. A Roman household proverb for extreme pliancy. Keeping the diminutive ("little ear-tip") preserves the affectionate exaggeration.

Perseus prints "eximus iudiciis," plainly a typographic corruption. Reading eximiis (so Watt OCT, Shackleton Bailey): Caesar's outstanding/exceptional judgements about young Crassus, conveyed (perhaps in correspondence) and known to Cicero.

alternatives: set down in Greek letters
record in Greek script
note: litterae Graecae here means Greek-language writing/literature, not just Greek script: Apollonius proposes a prose history of Caesar's wars in the Hellenistic-celebratory tradition (cf. Theophanes of Mytilene on Pompey).

alternatives: you are in my reckoning
you count among my own
I have you on my account
note: The idiom takes its image from the account-ledger (aes); a modern English idiom that keeps the bookkeeping image is preferable to a paraphrase like "in my circle." "On my books" reads cleanly and preserves the ledger metaphor that Cicero builds on with magno cumulo ("with a great heap") at the close.
